nativeRange.habitat: The Striped Headstander inhabits fast-flowing rocky rivers and streams with strong current, taking shelter among rocks, submerged wood, and dense riparian vegetation. It frequently occupies crevices and grazes on aufwuchs growing on rocky surfaces.

decorPreferences: large smooth rocks and boulders for territory and grazing surfaces
driftwood and roots providing line-of-sight breaks
strong current from powerheads or circulation pumps
avoid live plants — will be destroyed

diet.notes: The Striped Headstander is a heavily plant-based omnivore. In the wild, its diet centers on aufwuchs — the layer of algae, biofilm, and associated microorganisms scraped from rock surfaces — supplemented with plant matter, insects, and small invertebrates. In the aquarium it generally accepts spirulina wafers, blanched vegetables such as zucchini, cucumber, spinach, and lettuce, algae-based sinking pellets, and high-quality vegetable flakes. Frozen or live foods such as brine shrimp, bloodworm, and daphnia are also taken readily. Soft-leaved aquatic plants will be consumed and destroyed, so plastic or hardscape-only décor is strongly advised. Offering small amounts two or three times daily, with a vegetable supplement at least once per day, helps keep the fish active and in good condition.

goodWith: large robust characins (e.g., silver dollars, Buenos Aires tetras)
large peaceful cichlids with similar water requirements
fast-moving mid-water fish too large to be fin-nipped
avoidWith: conspecifics unless kept in groups of 6 or more (intense intraspecific aggression in groups of 2–5)
long-finned species (angelfish, bettas, fancy goldfish — fins will be damaged)
delicate or soft-leaved plants (will be destroyed)
dwarf cichlids and similarly-sized territorial fish
invertebrates small enough to be harassed

imageCandidates.notes: Brian Gratwicke; 3000x2000. Sharp, whole fish in frame in a planted tank, clearly showing the golden-and-black horizontal lateral stripes, red dorsal/caudal/anal/pelvic fins, and characteristic head-down angle.
Brian Gratwicke; ~1024 large. Striking black-background studio shot, sharp, whole fish, diagnostic gold/black stripes and red fins; different look for variety.
Jutta234, Zoo Frankfurt; 1848x1250. Different individual with vivid red caudal fin and gold/black striping; slightly soft/motion-blurred so kept as non-recommended extra.

summary: A striking 15–18 cm Amazonian characin with three bold black horizontal stripes on a yellow-orange body and a distinctive 45° head-down swimming posture. An advanced community fish best suited to experienced keepers: it destroys plants, nips fins, and shows intense aggression toward its own kind unless kept singly or in groups of six or more.
careNotes:
The Striped Headstander (Anostomus anostomus) is one of the most visually distinctive freshwater fish in the hobby, immediately recognizable by three broad black horizontal stripes on a vivid yellow-orange body and a characteristic 45-degree head-down swimming angle used to graze algae and biofilm from rock surfaces and wood. Adults reach 15–18 cm, requiring a spacious tank of at least 300 L with a footprint of 150 cm or longer.

Water quality should reflect its Amazonian and Orinoco origins: soft to moderately hard (gH 2–15), slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5), warm (22–28°C), and well-oxygenated with a strong current. Regular water changes of 30–50% weekly are important to keep nitrates low. Dim to moderate lighting is preferred.

The Striped Headstander is an aggressive plant-eater and will methodically destroy even robust-leaved aquatic plants. A hardscape-only setup with rocks, boulders, and driftwood is strongly recommended. Algae growth on rocks and wood is beneficial and appreciated as natural grazing material.

Conspecific aggression is a key management challenge. In groups of 2–5, dominant fish will relentlessly harass subordinates, leading to stress, injury, and death. The species should be kept either singly or in groups of six or more, where aggression is distributed and no single fish bears the brunt. Even in large groups, close monitoring during the initial establishment period is advised.

This species is also a confirmed fin-nipper and should not be housed with slow-moving, delicate, or long-finned fish. Robust fast-moving companions — large characins, armored catfish, sizable cichlids — are the safest tankmates.

Do not confuse with the Spotted Headstander (Chilodus punctatus, fw-fish-229), which belongs to a different family (Chilodidae), reaches only 8–9 cm, is genuinely peaceful, and can be kept in groups without the aggression management concerns that apply to Anostomus anostomus.
breedingNotes: Breeding in the home aquarium is rarely achieved and is considered very difficult. The species has been bred in very large aquaria under specific conditions approximating the rainy-season trigger: a slight drop in temperature, introduction of soft, acidic water, and increased water flow. Eggs are scattered and non-adhesive; adults will consume eggs if not removed. No parental care is exhibited. Fry are extremely small and require infusoria or commercial fry foods initially. Commercial supply is almost entirely wild-caught.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Anostomus anostomus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 18 cm), temperature range, pH, hardness, native range (Amazon and Orinoco basins), biotope, diet (aufwuchs-based omnivore), conspecific aggression management (singly or 6+), fin-nipping behavior, and plant destruction warnings. Confirmed family Anostomidae, order Characiformes.

additional.name: FishBase — Anostomus anostomus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 17.8 cm TL), temperature (22–28°C), native range (Amazon, Orinoco, coastal rivers of Guianas and Trinidad), diet (omnivore — algae, plant matter, invertebrates), taxonomy (Anostomidae, Characiformes), and depth/habitat data. Confirmed head-down posture and striking stripe pattern.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Headstander Fish Care
additional.notes: Trade-oriented care guide. Confirmed intermediate care level, minimum tank size guidance (~75 gal / ~280 L for a single specimen, larger for groups), diet (heavy plant-based), plant destruction warning, fin-nipping caution, and conspecific aggression management (keep singly or 6+). Water temperature 74–82°F (23–28°C), pH 6.0–7.5 cited.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Anostomus anostomus profile
additional.notes: UK trade source confirming 45-degree head-down swimming posture, stripe coloration on yellow-orange body, aggressive plant-eating, strong fin-nipping tendency, and high intraspecific aggression. Recommended tankmates limited to robust fast-moving fish. Noted that this species is distinct from the peaceful Spotted Headstander (Chilodus punctatus).

additional.name: Fishbase — Anostomus anostomus (additional distribution)
additional.notes: Additional country and distribution data cross-check. Confirmed presence in Brazil, Colombia, Venezuela, Guyana, and Peru. Breeding difficulty confirmed as not documented in captivity under standard aquarium conditions; wild-caught supply predominates the trade.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is rarely achieved and requires a very large aquarium with rainy-season conditioning — a slight temperature drop, an influx of soft acidic water, and increased flow. Eggs are scattered and non-adhesive; adults must be removed promptly to prevent predation. Fry are extremely small and require infusoria or liquid fry food initially. Commercial stock is almost entirely wild-caught.
